sentences.text: Yes.
Carlei was his name.
And he knew the Archbishop of Slovakia.
And they cooked the goose.
My father, they had to give it, outrageous donation.
And so they packed me.
My mother was packing a month, with one suitcase, with everything which was permitted.
And I went, in 1942, when I was 15 years of age, to Trencin.

Suddenly, I was in a convent full of Jewish children, where little ones, some of them quite small, where their parents left money and jewelry and everything for the nuns to take care of the children until they come back.
Beautiful.
It was a-- it was a big project, with a cathedral.
I was singing in the choir, 'Tantum Ergo Sacramentum,' I still remember.
The nuns made no effort of conversion, which so many people think they did.
On the contrary, we had to sit through Jewish scripture.
They insisted on that.
And on Sunday, when everybody else went walking, I had to sit though the Old Testament.
That was my first Jewish scripture lesson in my life, from Catholic nuns.
And they-- they-- they were very touched to help these children.
I don't think they knew very much about the money or everything.
But then, in '43, things are starting to get tough.
And many-- they had a bush telegraph going.
Everything was grapevine, like in Auschwitz.
Everything was grapevine, because there was nothing, no newspapers, no nothing.
So they had this bush telegraph that they are catching Jews.
And then we had to sleep next to the altar, in the sacristy, it's called.
And the sisters were sitting there, who were on duty that night.
And you heard, on top, were the heavy boots of the Gestapo.
And the Slovak Hlinka Guard is walking up and down.
And I thought, this was exciting.
I thought to myself, what is going to help me if they break in here and if they catch us?
And the sisters must have died of fear.
They were sitting with their rosary, and they prayed.
I now understand what angst means.
But I didn't then.
It was hidden.
It was a trapdoor.
And it was a convent.
Nobody knew there were Jewish children there.
Who should have told them?
Nobody knew.
Maybe six.
Maybe eight.
Maybe-- I don't remember.
The little ones, I remember, Evichka, a little girl.
Terrible.
Their parents-- none of these children survived.
And the parents never came back and the whole thing.
Every second Sunday, I was allowed to go home.
I didn't wear a Star of David, at the time, anymore, because my father didn't wear a Star of David, because the Deutsche Wehrmacht sheltered him, because he was a highly decorated officer.
So he did come under the jurisdiction of the Army and not of the SS.
And that's why we survived till 1944, when after the Stauffenberg putsch on Hitler's life, came the Eichmann's Final Solution.
And they caught baptized ones and one-legged ones and Star of David and not Star of David.
And one night, we were sitting there freezing in that-- it was September, around about the Jewish holidays, but I was in a convent.
I only felt, somehow, that they must be close to the Jewish holy days, by the virtue that it was September.
And there was a terrible shooting, suddenly, against the door.
And they shot a hole, very quickly, and opened from the inside.
And in marched the leather coats, the Gestapo, the Slovakian Hlinka Guards.
Hands up, everybody.
The sisters blessed themselves.
And I thought to myself, next chapter of my book.
This is going to be exciting.
I did not know about Auschwitz.
I never heard that there were deportations.
I knew that people were going to work, to Germany, somewhere, into forced labor.
So I was all psyched up.
I'm going to this forced labor camp.
And there will be all sorts of interesting people to talk to.
And I wasn't afraid for one minute of my life.
It makes up now when I suffer from very nasty and very painful angst and anxieties, because of my nervous condition.
And-- So there were the famous black trucks outside the convent.
That was a-- that was a miserable moment, because they were so high.
And they let the nuns go.
The nuns could go.
I visited them after the war.
And they took the Jewish children.
Everybody who had parents went back to his parents.
So on the 11th of September, 1944, I came under SS guard into my parents' home.
My father nearly freaked out, because he thought I was expelled out of school.
He didn't think, my-- to my father, the thought that somebody should come and arrest him was as far away, further than my own, further.
Was that because of the fact that he was protected by the Wehrmacht?
Yes, and because he was a-- a powerhouse.
He did nothing wrong to nobody.
He was a very talented, very brilliant man.
And he didn't believe-- he knew-- he read.
He listened to London.
He knew that-- that the invasion started.
They knew that the ebb started.
They knew that they slowly, slowly closing on-- on Germany, on Dunkirk.
He knew all this.
So he could work it out.
He did not know that the Russians in Yalta, they have asked to stop until they all meet in Berlin.
We-- they could have been alive.
Not my father, he's too old for that.
So, I am marched home.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they left.
Kitty, can I ask just one question?
Amongst the news of the war and of the Allies, was there any news about Auschwitz or Eastern Europe?
Never.
Never, never, never.
But don't forget, I was a child.
If there was, they never told me.
But I never knew.
And my parents didn't know, for sure.
They thought, we go for work.
We had the rucksacks packed, 25 kilos.
And--
They were there, paranoiacally, since 1942, already packed.
And my mother made a Hungarian fruitcake called, bischofsbrot, bishop's bread.
Every Friday, she made it fresh, took the stale one out from the week before.
And we all rejoiced that we could eat it at home.
This stale cake is still in my-- in my psychological make up.
I like rotten things.
I like stale sings.
I like secondhand clothes.
I like all these things, which came in my early, formative years-- I kept.
I didn't-- I haven't shed any of these traumas of these times.
I collect it only more and more on top of it.
On the 12th.
Next morning, on the 13th of September, knock, knock, knock.
My father was having breakfast, dry zwieback and tea, because he had to watch his weight, because he had only one leg that he-- it's not over-strained.
In comes a boy, Schevo was his name, who was selling ice cream in this street.
Schevo, Such a nobody, I wouldn't-- I didn't even look at him when he walked in.
Who is Schevo?
He had the armband of the Hlinka Guards, which are now very favored again, in Slovakia, of course.
And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mister-- he said, pan-- he said, Mr Haas, in the name of the Republic, you and your family come with me.
You are under arrest.
So my father, who spoke only Czech and not Slovak, which irritated them no end, turned around and said, can't you see I'm having my breakfast, and didn't blink.
I left the kitchen, where it all happened, and went into the bedroom and opened my cupboard and started to put on six panties.
I had little bras back then-- no, quite big bras, already, these days, so three bras.
And I looked what clothing I could put on, a sailor dress, because I thought, if we're going to work in Germany, maybe they will not allow us to wash.
So I have plenty of change.
I was completely oblivion, what happened to my mother, what happened to my father, what happened to my sister.
I looked after myself.
I still think back of that moment.
I'm-- the rucksacks were packed.
My father couldn't carry.
So very proudly, I carried it myself.
And then they looked through the rest of the flat, what there hasn't been plundered yet, the Slovak.
And they saw the piano was too big.
They couldn't do anything about it.
But they saw the cello in the corner of the music room.
And they took the cello, the Gestapo.
And outside was a stupid urchin called Mischko-- Mischko And they gave Mischko the cello.
And Mischko swapped it for a mouth organ with somebody.
And that was the end of the musical career of my father.
The Germans.
The Germans, who took over somehow, they came.
I don't know.
They came.
In the convent, too, in their leather coats.
That's how they suddenly started to open.
That was after Stauffenberg.
That was the Final Solution.
Everybody with Gestapo everywhere.
Geheime Staatspolizei, secret police.
Yes, yes, yes.
They were called, Hlinka Guards, black uniforms, black jackboots, and the double-- double cross on the left armband, red, white, blue, still very fashionable, today, in Slovakia, very fashionable.
To the local police station.
I thought it was very amusing.
To the police station, where there were already quite a few very shocked people.
There were only two young people there besides me and my sister.
All the others were elderly people, who were all somehow sheltered from deportation.
There was the economically indispensable Jew, like my father.
For that, you had to pay 1 million Czech crown to President Tiso in a-- in a roundabout way.
And they were all there, sitting there, very shocked.
I thought to myself, what is there to be shocked about?
The Russians are in the Vistula.
They'll be here in a fortnight.
Then I started to talk.
I met this girl, Gaby-- she's here, Gaby Hirschman-- Gorsky, now, her name-- and her son-- brother, Paul, a young man.
And that was my first love.
We went hand in hand.
We were taken from there the same day to Ilava, which is a high-security jail, a shocking concentration camp for Slovak criminals.
So they opened a cell.
Up there was a little window.
It was dark.
And there was a untidy woman, with a red-ringed eye, sitting on wet straw.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] such a derelict.
And I introduced myself.
And I-- I've tried to shake hands.
She wouldn't.
And I said to her, why are you here?
She looked at me, and she said, I got 25 years for murder.
I thought, my book, my book, the first murderer in my life.
I said, excuse me, whom did you murder?
She said, I knifed my husband.
She was made-- she was made, in my imagination, such a brave girl.
She goes and knifes a miserable drunkard.
God knows what a bastard he was.
And I-- as I didn't even start the conversation with her, when they opened the thick doors, took us all out there.
It was a mistake.
We were not supposed to go there, but to Sered-- S-E-R-E-D for David.
The lot.
Maybe 20.
That was all.
The lot.
Not the majority, the lot.
That was the lot.
They hang-- hung out the white flag when we were gone, Judenrein.
The bastards.

So anyhow, we come to Sered, where this horrible Alois Brunner-- who is now in Damascus, and they can't get him out-- in a snow-white, SS Obersturmbannfuehrer uniform, having-- That was my first concentration camp.
How did you get there?
By-- on the black truck.
From Ilava.
Somewhere in Slovakia, God knows.
Near-- probably nearer to the Hungarian border.
I have no idea of conception, and I'm not interested.
And I-- we only heard that we were there.
And it was quite pleasant, I thought.
We were so many people, maybe 100 in one barrack.
Suddenly, the overflow-- this was always a concentration camp.
And they built houses there.
And they prepared them for Auschwitz.
And it was just one of these transit camps.
And suddenly, it-- we-- we were too much for them.
They were not geared up for so many people.
So people had to go up for work.
My mother volunteered for it.
I still think, as I talk back of her, how she adapted.
That must be Jewish, this.
I lived in a dream world.
To me, this was only material for my book.
I didn't give a continental what was happening to anybody else.
My mother volunteered in the laundry.
She has never seen a laundry from inside in her life.
And there was a Mr.
Pokorny.
This Pokorny was there, because he, by a fluke of bad luck for him, he was, like very many quislings, denouncing Jews who were in hiding.
And in the end, they caught him.
And they brought him to Sered and made him manager of the laundry.
Oh, yes.
To go-- be ready to go to Auschwitz.
And in Auschwitz, they burned him alive.
They waited for him.
They knew in which transportation, the ones whom he sent there.
Pokorny, a very famous figure in Jewish persecution in Slovakia.
P for Paul, O-K-O-R-N-Y, Pokorny.
And where he was cleaning there in the laundry, my mother worked in the laundry, a few others.
She made friends with Jewish women she never met, she never knew.
They were talking about their experiences.
I was stunned.
My mother, from that arrogant Mrs.
Haas, became just somebody with a great big Star of David, got up at 6 o'clock in the morning.
And she had a bad limp, one leg shorter than the other.
And my poor father could not go anywhere.
One day, they had this wonderful idea.
Would everybody over 60 step out?
Over 60-- my father-- or maybe over 55, because my father would have been in that category.
Maybe they meant 55.
I don't remember.
I went there instead of him.
They said, what are you doing here?
You're not 55.
You know where these people are going?
I said, I don't care.
But my father is not going.
I go.
I argued with Mr.
Brunner.
He-- I had not a [NON-ENGLISH].
I never knew fear.
They didn't bother me, what they were doing.
I was doing my own thing.
That was very strong in me in these days.
I did my thing.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Auschwitz, when they shave-- shaved us with the clippers, so when he did my pubic hair, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a Polish kapo-- kapo for katzetpolizei, K-A-P-O, the feared, feared helpers of the Nazis.
And this kapo, I pissed on him, because I couldn't help it.
Nobody ever touched me there before in my life.
Maybe my mother when she gave me a bath.
And he said, why-- why did you do that, when he hit me.
I said, if you do this once more, I complain to the International Red Cross.
And he looked at me.
He was scared of me.
But he kept shaving, don't worry.
But we are not there yet.
We are serious, bloody Sereds.
Good.
I have good memories.
We had black coffee, one big thing, a container, with black-- it wasn't even coffee, it was bitter, terrible stuff.
But it was liquid.
It was early in the morning.
And it was one mark.
The system was always the same, this dehumanization.
There it started.
For 20 people, 1 mark.
For me to drink after other people, what I was used to from home, that was that very hard for me.
That I remember.
Hygiene, yes, we could shower.
We had soap.
We brought all this with us.
And then came [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yom Kippur, in Sered, in 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And since the people who knew how to pray, prayed, people were singing the Kol Nidre, nobody stopped-- stopped us.
I did not know what they were doing, because I did not know any Kol Nidre.
And I didn't know any Yom Kippur.
And then came the famous story, which 100 people before me said and will say, that there was such a wonderful smell all over the camp.
And they called us in at lunchtime.
There was always such a gong.
And they called us.
And we went.
And they roasted a pig, a huge, beautiful pig.
And they said come on, Jews, your big holiday today.
You should see how humane we are to you.
And do you know, we were standing there, and that is the first time I was proud that I am Jewish.
And maybe the only time in my life.
But that was the first time that nobody went forward, nobody in this empty stomach.
And it would have been, Hasidically and Halalically, absolutely permissible under such circumstances.
But-- and there I realized, slowly, in my imagination, what the tradition means, like the 'Tradition.' To me, it wasn't a religious thing.
It was tradition.
You don't eat on Yom Kippur.
You don't eat on Yom Kippur, finish.
But it was somehow-- I felt there was more to it than just not eating.
Today, I know.
And then, suddenly, the panic, transportations are commencing.
And in the first transportation, 1st of October, 1944, we were put in, because they cleaned out the camp, cleaned out the camp.
From the-- from the 15th of September to the 30th of September, a fortnight.
And I said to-- I went with this Pole, he was with me in the wagon.
There were scenes, which I don't forget.
There was a Mr and Mrs Barna, an old, delightful couple.
He had a removal store in Trencin, where I went to boarding school.
And his only daughter married a doctor, Oskar Smetala.
Dr.
Oskar Smetala was a great bridge player and a bad doctor, but he married a lot of money.
And they went, with the parents, into the wagon.
The old ones were so confused by that time, already, because they did not understand what was happening at all.
They must have been in their late 70s.
And suddenly, somebody pulled out Dr.
Smetala and said, you are a doctor.
You step out of line.
And Mrs.
Smetala went with him.
And she kissed her mother and patted her father.
And said, mamushka, don't worry.
I catch up with you in the next transportation.
I'll catch up.
And she let these old people go.
And she stayed back.
To me, it happened again, when we went in this cattle train, where they unloaded us on the 1st of October.
And I had to urgently go to the loo.

And not Slovaks were with us, but Hungarian Nyilas, Hungarian SS helped this transportation-- there were not enough Slovaks available-- the Schwabs from around Budapest, the terrible Szalasi Nazis, like the Hlinka Nazis.
And in a very shocking, miserable, German, he said to me, stay out.
He let me out.
He said, stay and wait till the train goes, and run away.
I said, I have my parents in there.
How can I run away?
Where-- where will I go?
Night-- it was in the middle of the night.
And he said, you will not see your parents after tomorrow anymore.
I run?
I was disgusted.
I will run away from my parents to leave them there in that stinking-- and then I still climbed in.
Was this the first time you had thought about your parents?
You say that, previously, you had thought--
The was, I think, the first time where I was so very concerned.
Maybe I wasn't so concerned about my parents, but about what is going to become of me without my parents.
Maybe this.
But this is only a rationalization.
I can't judge that.
Caring.
I look-- I organized them.
I looked after them.
I picked up the food.
I did all the running and shunting with my mother.
My father couldn't, with his one leg.
And my sister was a child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we come.
We sit in this train.
My father had to undo his wooden leg and hang it on the hook in the cattle train.
And that-- then tick, tock, tick, tock, for two days.
And I looked at it.
And I said to myself, this is my father saying goodbye to me.
I knew then.
I knew that this is going to be bad.
Nobody told me.
But I saw religious Jewish men taking out their kittel and getting dressed in their tachrichims.
And I said, what cowards.
They're preparing to be buried.
I asked one man what he has that?
And they were praying.
They were getting ready to die.
They knew somehow.
I thought to myself, why wait till you die?
Why don't you do something about it?
I'm going to do something about it.
I'm not going to die.
Not for one minute did I-- not for one second did I think that I'm not going to survive.
Because I had to write this book.
I had to do it.
And my mother was leaning against me, in that awful, stinking, terrible, cattle train, which was 80 people were in one, instead of 40, 80.
You couldn't move.
Two died, fortunately, so there was a bit more room.
People are people.
Throw them out on the next station.
Yes, many times.
Yes, and I know that there were 21 cars.
And there were over 2,100 people.
No.
I think they were also from Novaky, from Zilina, from other camps.
They just cleaned up.
They made a big clean up.
And if you escaped this big cleanup, on the 2nd of October, 1944, Auschwitz stopped gassing.
That's a fact.
If you would have made it, then you would have come to Theresienstadt.
Theresienstadt was survivable, because there was no gas anymore.
I mean if you survived, if you didn't get typhoid fever, if you didn't get all sorts of terrible diseases, cholera.
So--
